notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Here’s a simple way to think about backdoor Roth IRAs, and what info would help decide if it makes sense for you.

1. How a backdoor Roth IRA works

A backdoor Roth is just a two-step workaround if your income is too high to contribute directly to a Roth IRA:

Make a non-deductible Traditional IRA contribution

You put money into a Traditional IRA without taking a tax deduction.
Up to the annual IRA limit (2024: $7,000 per person, $8,000 if 50+).

Convert that Traditional IRA to a Roth IRA

Usually done soon after the contribution to minimize any growth in between.
You pay tax on any pre-tax money converted.
Once it’s in the Roth, future growth and qualified withdrawals are tax-free.

So the “backdoor” is just using the conversion route because direct Roth contributions are phased out at higher incomes.

2. The big pitfall: the pro-rata rule

When you convert to Roth, the IRS looks at all your Traditional, SEP, and SIMPLE IRAs together.

On December 31 of the conversion year, they total:

All pre-tax IRA money (deductible contributions + earnings)
All after-tax IRA money (non-deductible contributions you’ve reported on Form 8606)

Then they apply a pro-rata formula:

Tax-free portion of conversion = (after-tax basis ÷ total IRA balance) × amount converted
The rest of the conversion is taxable as ordinary income.

What that means for you:

If you have no other pre-tax IRA money, a backdoor Roth is usually clean and simple.
If you have large pre-tax IRA balances, most of your conversion will be taxable, which often makes the backdoor less attractive.

Common ways people handle this:

Roll pre-tax IRA money into a 401(k)/403(b) (if the plan accepts roll-ins and has decent options), so your IRA balances are mostly/entirely after-tax before doing backdoor contributions.
Skip backdoor Roths in years when you can’t clean up those pre-tax IRA balances.
3. Other common pitfalls / gotchas

Forgetting Form 8606

You must file Form 8606 to track non-deductible IRA contributions and basis.
Missing this can cause double taxation later.

Letting money sit in the Traditional IRA too long

If you contribute $7,000 and it grows to $7,500 before converting, that extra $500 is taxable at conversion.

Step-transaction concern

Some people worry about contributing and converting the same day. The IRS hasn’t explicitly banned this, but many leave a short gap (days/weeks) to be conservative. It’s more about comfort level than a clearly defined rule.

Married filing separately

IRA deduction and Roth rules are much harsher for MFS; this can affect whether a backdoor Roth is needed or how it’s treated.
4. Where backdoor Roths usually fit in the priority order

A common (not universal) tax-advantaged savings order looks like this:

Where the backdoor Roth fits:

It’s just the mechanism for step 4 when your income is too high for direct Roth contributions.

If you’re already:

Getting the full employer match
Handling high-interest debt
Using an HSA (if available)
And still have capacity to save more

then a backdoor Roth is often a strong next move.

If you haven’t yet maxed those earlier items (match, high-interest debt payoff, HSA), those usually come before a backdoor Roth.

5. When a backdoor Roth is often especially attractive

It tends to be compelling when:

Your income is above the Roth IRA contribution limits
You have little or no pre-tax IRA money (so the pro-rata rule doesn’t hurt you)
You expect to be in a similar or higher tax bracket later, so Roth dollars are valuable
You’ve already hit the key priorities (match, high-interest debt, HSA, etc.)

It’s less attractive when:

You have large pre-tax IRA balances you can’t roll into a 401(k)/403(b)
You’re in a very high tax bracket now but expect a much lower one in retirement (traditional/pre-tax savings may be more appealing)
You’re still working on an emergency fund or paying down high-interest debt
6. What I’d need to tailor this for you

To say whether you specifically should prioritize a backdoor Roth vs other moves, it would help to know:

Income and filing status

Your and partner’s approximate gross annual income
Filing status (single, married filing jointly, married filing separately, etc.)
Whether each of you is covered by a workplace plan (401(k)/403(b)/TSP/etc.)

Current retirement accounts and balances

Whether either of you has:
Traditional IRAs
Rollover IRAs
SEP or SIMPLE IRAs
Rough balances in each (especially pre-tax IRAs, because of the pro-rata rule)
Existing Roth IRAs and whether you’re currently contributing directly

Current contribution levels

How much each of you is contributing per year to:
401(k)/403(b)/TSP (and what the employer match is)
HSA (if any)
IRAs (Roth or Traditional)
Whether your 401(k) allows:
After-tax contributions beyond the pre-tax/Roth limit
In-plan Roth conversions or in-service rollovers (for a potential mega backdoor Roth)

Debt and cash priorities

Any high-interest debt (credit cards, personal loans, etc.) and approximate interest rates
Whether you have at least a basic emergency fund (e.g., 1–3 months of expenses)

Time horizon and goals

Rough ages for e
